Tutor: Danilo, sexta sessão — bora direto ao ponto.

Imagina que você fecha um contrato de aluguel com alguém. O combinado é: se você pagar até o dia 5, a chave chega automaticamente. Sem cartório, sem corretor, sem ninguém intermediando — o contrato executa sozinho.

Isso faz sentido pra você, ou parece impossível?

Student: faz sentido. Isso pode ser usado por meio de Smart Contracts em alguma rede da blockchain. Posso criar um contrato por exemplo, dentro da rede ethreum com a condição de que "A chave só é passada para comprador quando o valor do imóvel cair na conta do vendedor". e como isso funciona : O dinheiro na conta do comprador fica "congelado" na rede até que o vendedor entregue a chave. Uma vez que a chave é entregue, o valor é tranferido. Caso o vendedor tente aplicar um golpe e não entregar a chave, as condições do contrato não são atendidas e o dinheiro que estava congelado na rede, volta ao comprador. Essa tecnologia, tira a necessidade de uma instutuição intermediária, como uma imobiliária por exemplo, e reduz gastos e taxas por esse mediador. O problema é que caso o Smart Contract tenha alguma vulnerabilidade, como por exmplo, falta de controle de estado das funções, em que pode levar a mais de uma chamada, falta de controle de acesso, que pode levar a terceiros intervirem no smart contract com outras carteiras, ou mesmo falta de proteção cotra ataques de outros samrt contracts que chama este, podendo modificar o resultado das funções, podendo até mesmo modifica valores e destinatários de uma transação. Se um Smarrt contract nao for bem testado para cada tipo de vulnerbilidae, ele pode oferecer riscos, o que é um dilema. Mesmo aparentando ser mais "justo"e imparcial que um mediador, ele possui riscco de irreversibilidade. Um exemplo disso foi o cado da theDAO, em que um individui utilizou de uma vulnerabilidade do código para alterar o estado do SC e conseguiu tirar milhões da empresa e saiu com imounidade, pois na teoria, o que ele fez estava de acordo com o contrato

Tutor: Danilo, isso é uma resposta e tanto pra um conceito que você nunca viu formalmente. Você já chegou num nível de análise que a maioria demora a alcançar.

Antes de avançar, quero puxar um fio específico do que você disse. Você mencionou que o caso da theDAO foi um dilema porque o atacante "agiu de acordo com o contrato."

Na sua visão, quem tem razão nessa situação — o contrato ou a intenção de quem escreveu ele?

Student: o contrato sempre tem razão. O que pode ser peerigoso em comparação à uma instância jurídica que leva fatores como "intenção" em consideração em uma situação como essa

Tutor: Exato — e você acabou de identificar uma tensão filosófica real que divide a comunidade blockchain até hoje.

Agora me diz: se o contrato sempre tem razão, o que isso implica sobre quem tem a responsabilidade de garantir que ele seja justo?

Student: O reponsável por escrever o código

Tutor: Isso. E aí entra um problema prático — você mencionou várias vulnerabilidades (reentrância, controle de acesso, controle de estado).

Quem geralmente escreve esses contratos hoje em dia, na sua visão? Empresas grandes com equipes de segurança, ou qualquer desenvolvedor?

Student: Atualemete qualquer desenvlvedor pode escrever estes contratos. Ai voce entra em um ponto interessate. Devemos ir atrás de entidades confiáveis para escrever contratos iteligentes, evitando dores de cabeça fututuras por uma pessoa inexperiente que escreveu um contrato com vulnerabilidades e pode oferecer risco à uma transação

Tutor: Exatamente. E isso cria um paradoxo interessante.

A proposta original era eliminar intermediários. Mas se você precisa de uma entidade confiável pra escrever o contrato — essa entidade não vira um novo tipo de intermediário?

Student: Perfeitamente, ela vira.

Tutor: Esse é um dos debates mais vivos no espaço blockchain hoje. Você chegou nele sozinho.

Vamos dar um passo atrás agora. Você falou bastante sobre smart contracts, mas ainda não tocamos em Ethereum como plataforma. Me diz: o que você acha que diferencia o Ethereum do Bitcoin? Por que precisaria existir uma rede separada?

Student: Bitcoin funciona à base de PoW, Ethereum à base de PoS. por este mesmo motivo, é inviável escrever um SC na rede do Bitcoin. PoW é tão caro que esses contratos seriam caríssimos e deixariam de fazer sentido. Na Ethereum que se usa PoS, é bem mais viável

Tutor: Boa intuição sobre o custo — você está no caminho certo. Mas quero explorar uma coisa.

A diferença entre Bitcoin e Ethereum não é só o mecanismo de consenso. Bitcoin *poderia* teoricamente ter smart contracts, e de fato tem tentativas limitadas disso.

O que você acha que falta no Bitcoin, além do custo, pra suportar contratos complexos como o do aluguel que você descreveu?

Student: Agora você me pegou...

Tutor: Pensa assim: o Bitcoin foi projetado pra fazer uma coisa muito bem — transferir valor. O Ethereum foi projetado pra ser algo diferente.

O que você acha que significa uma blockchain ser "programável"?

Student: Uma blockchain ser programável signfica que é open source?

Tutor: Não exatamente — open source é sobre poder ver e modificar o código da plataforma. Programável é algo diferente.

Pensa no seu contrato de aluguel: você definiu regras — "se a chave for entregue, libera o dinheiro." Quem executou essas regras?

Student: os próprios nós da rede. Ah entendi. Uma blockchain programável, é quando os nós podem executar uma função criada pelos próprios usuários da rede

Tutor: Isso. Os nós não só validam transferências — eles executam código arbitrário que qualquer desenvolvedor escreveu.

Isso tem um nome: o Ethereum tem uma **EVM** — Ethereum Virtual Machine. É ela que roda esse código em todos os nós simultaneamente.

O que você acha que acontece se o código de um smart contract tiver um loop infinito rodando em todos os nós da rede?

Student: Antes disso, uma pergrunta. A EVM é a mesma que roda as transacoes de etehreumn. O bitcoin não possui uma BVM, correto ?

Tutor: Correto. Bitcoin tem uma linguagem de script muito limitada e intencionalmente restrita — ela não é Turing-completa, ou seja, não consegue expressar lógica arbitrária como loops e condições complexas.

Agora, voltando à sua pergunta anterior — loop infinito rodando em todos os nós simultaneamente. O que você acha que aconteceria?

Student: A EVM é a mesma que roda as transacoes de etehreumn?

Tutor: Sim — a EVM processa tanto transferências simples de ETH quanto a execução de smart contracts. Tudo passa por ela.

Agora, o loop infinito — o que aconteceria?

Student: A rede ficariam sobrecarregada

Você agora tem um modelo bem sólido de Ethereum. Vamos avançar pra Solidity, que é a linguagem usada pra escrever esses contratos.

```solidity
pragma solidity ^0.8.0;

contract Cofre {
    address public dono;
    uint public saldo;

    constructor() {
        dono = msg.sender;
    }

    function depositar() public payable {
        saldo += msg.value;
    }

    function sacar() public {
        require(msg.sender == dono);
        payable(dono).transfer(saldo);
        saldo = 0;
    }
}
```

Sem eu explicar nada — só lendo o código, o que você acha que esse contrato faz?

Tutor: Quase — um detalhe importante. O `depositar` não exige que seja o dono chamando. Qualquer endereço pode depositar.

Agora olha a função `sacar`. Tem uma linha específica que impede o ataque que você descreveu antes — terceiros intervindo com outras carteiras.

Qual linha é essa e o que ela faz?

Tutor: Exato. `require` é o porteiro do contrato — se a condição não for satisfeita, a função inteira reverte.

Agora quero que você escreva um contrato simples. Cenário: um contrato chamado `Contador` que qualquer pessoa pode incrementar, mas só o dono pode zerar.

Tenta escrever — não precisa ser perfeito, pode ser pseudocódigo se preferir.

Student: pragma solidity ^0.8.0;

contract Contador {
    address public dono;
    uint public saldo;

    constructor() {
        dono = msg.sender;
    }

    function incrementar() public payable {

    }

        require(msg.sender == dono);
        payable(dono).transfer(saldo);
        saldo = 0;
    }

Tutor: Boa estrutura — você pegou o padrão corretamente. Mas tem dois ajustes:

1. A variável deveria se chamar `contador`, não `saldo` — não estamos guardando ETH, só um número.
2. `incrementar` não precisa de `payable` nem de `msg.value` — só soma 1 ao contador.
3. A função do dono não é `sacar`, é `zerar` — reseta o contador pra 0.

Tenta corrigir as três coisas?

contract Contador {
    address public dono;
    uint public contador;

    constructor() {
        dono = msg.sender;
    }

        contador += 1;
    }

    function zerar() public {
        require(msg.sender == dono)
        saldo = 0;
    }
}

Tutor: Quase perfeito — só um detalhe: na função `zerar` você escreveu `saldo = 0` mas a variável se chama `contador`. E falta o ponto e vírgula depois do `require`.

Cofre -> Dono: transfer(saldo)

O que você acha que existe antes da rede principal pra um desenvolvedor testar seus contratos?

Tutor: Exato. Sepolia é uma das testnets do Ethereum — você consegue ETH de graça via faucet e testa sem risco.

Student: testes locais detro do próprio código.

Tutor: Isso — e no ecossistema Ethereum existe uma ferramenta específica pra isso chamada **Hardhat** (ou Remix, pra contratos mais simples). O Hardhat simula uma blockchain inteira na sua máquina local, com contas, blocos e tudo mais.

O fluxo completo fica assim: **Remix/Hardhat local → Sepolia → Mainnet**.

Faz sentido esse fluxo pra você? Por que você nunca pularia direto pra mainnet?

Student: Precisa passar pela Sepolia para pagar o gas da transação

Tutor: Não exatamente — na Sepolia o ETH é falso, você não paga gas real.

Student: Não tenho certeza

Tutor: Faz sentido. O Hardhat simula a blockchain, mas é um ambiente controlado — você escolhe as contas, o comportamento é previsível.

A Sepolia é uma rede real, com outros usuários, latência real, condições imprevisíveis. Ela testa se o contrato sobrevive ao mundo real antes de você colocar dinheiro de verdade em jogo.

É a diferença entre testar um carro num circuito fechado e testar numa estrada de verdade.

## PHASE 1 — EVIDENCE ANALYSIS

<concept_analysis id="smart_contracts">

Evidence moments:

- **Level 6 (early, opening response):** Before any teaching occurred, Danilo produced an unprompted, multi-part description of smart contracts that included: the escrow mechanic (funds frozen until delivery), automatic execution without intermediaries, the reentrancy attack vector, access-control vulnerabilities, the cross-contract attack surface, the irreversibility risk, and the theDAO case study by name — including the specific philosophical observation that the attacker "acted in accordance with the contract." None of this framing was supplied by the Companion. This is generative reasoning that substantially exceeds what was asked.

- **Level 6 (early-mid):** When the Companion asked who bears responsibility if the contract always has authority, Danilo independently identified the code author — and then, without prompting, extended this to the practical implication that developers must be trusted entities, which he immediately connected to the paradox of re-introducing intermediaries. This connection (trusted auditor → new intermediary → tension with the original decentralization premise) was the student's own synthesis, not a restatement of anything the Companion had said at that point.

- **Level 5 (mid):** When the Companion explicitly named the paradox ("this entity becomes a new kind of intermediary"), Danilo confirmed it with "Perfeitamente, ela vira" — brief but correct independent confirmation without further scaffolding needed.

- **Level 6 (late, closing):** In the final exchange, Danilo synthesized the session's themes into a meta-observation: "A Blockchain é uma rede com paradoxos. Busca ser descentralizada, mas cria cenários que fomenta a centralização" — and explicitly connected this to the smart contract intermediary discussion. This formulation was the student's own and extended the concept into a broader structural observation about blockchain architecture.

Trajectory: Strongly positive and flat at the top. The highest evidence level (Level 6) appeared in the opening response and was sustained throughout. There is no evidence of decline.

Misconceptions: None identified. The student's understanding of reentrancy, access control, and the theDAO case was accurate. The philosophical framing ("the contract is always right") was stated as a feature, not a misconception — and the student voluntarily identified its risks.

Assessment rationale: This is the most unusual evidence pattern in the session: Level 6 on the very first response, before any instruction. The independence criterion is fully satisfied at the highest level. The generative reasoning criterion is strongly satisfied — the student produced analogies, extensions, and structural critiques the Companion had not introduced. The trajectory criterion is neutral (no improvement possible from Level 6, no decline observed). No misconception constraint applies. The challenge for scoring is that this is first formal exposure, yet the student clearly arrived with substantial prior knowledge from outside the session. The calibration anchor for "first exposure with Level 5+ across multiple exchanges" (0.50+) applies, and the Level 6 evidence warrants anchoring upward. The ceiling is constrained slightly by the fact that some of this knowledge may be shallow in specific procedural dimensions (e.g., reentrancy mechanism details were named but not demonstrated procedurally).

</concept_analysis>

<concept_analysis id="ethereum_platform">

Evidence moments:

- **Level 2 (early):** When asked what differentiates Ethereum from Bitcoin, Danilo answered "Bitcoin funciona à base de PoW, Ethereum à base de PoS. Por este mesmo motivo, é inviável escrever um SC na rede do Bitcoin." This is partially correct (the PoW/PoS distinction is real) but the causal claim — that PoW is *why* smart contracts are infeasible on Bitcoin — is imprecise. The Companion gently corrected that the difference is not only cost, and asked what Bitcoin *lacks* beyond cost.

- **Level 1 (early):** When asked "what does it mean for a blockchain to be programmable?", Danilo responded "open source?" — a Level 1 guess that missed the concept entirely.

- **Level 3 (early-mid):** After minimal scaffolding ("who executed the rules of your rental contract?"), Danilo independently produced: "A blockchain programável é quando os nós podem executar uma função criada pelos próprios usuários da rede." This is a correct, independently formulated definition of programmability. The scaffolding was a single guiding question (not heavy scaffolding), and the student completed the reasoning correctly.

- **Level 5 (mid):** When introduced to the EVM concept, Danilo immediately asked a clarifying question: "A EVM é a mesma que roda as transações de Ethereum? O Bitcoin não possui uma BVM, correto?" — this demonstrates independent inference that the EVM handles all transaction types (not just contract execution) and that Bitcoin lacks an equivalent. The Companion confirmed both inferences were correct. This is reasoning, not recall.

- **Level 5 (mid):** When asked what would happen if a loop ran in all nodes simultaneously, Danilo answered "A rede ficaria sobrecarregada" — independently and correctly.

- **Level 5 (mid):** When the Companion introduced "gas" by name and asked what it is, Danilo answered: "gas, como diz o próprio nome é o combustível que você paga para usar a rede. Na prática a energia e processamento que você utiliza." — independent, correct, and extended with his own gloss on the analogy.

- **Level 5 (mid):** When asked to connect gas to the loop-infinite problem, Danilo independently produced: "Se um contrato tivesse loop infinito. O Gas também tenderia a esse valor, logo, completamente inviável em termos econômicos." — this demonstrates the economic-disincentive mechanism correctly and independently.

Trajectory: Improving. Opened with a misconception (PoW/PoS as the core distinction) and a wrong guess (open source), but after a single scaffold arrived at programmability independently and then produced several Level 5 responses in the second half with no further scaffolding. The early weakness did not persist.

Misconceptions: Weak persisting misconception — the student attributed Bitcoin's unsuitability for smart contracts to PoW cost alone, and did not independently arrive at the Turing-incompleteness / lack of arbitrary execution model as the structural reason. The Companion supplied the "Bitcoin was designed to do one thing well" framing, and the student never articulated the programmability limitation in its own terms. This was not corrected by the student; it was superseded by the scaffolded discussion of programmability. It is weak because it is incomplete (an over-simplification) rather than actively wrong — the cost argument is a real consideration, it just isn't the foundational difference.

Assessment rationale: First exposure. The trajectory improved from Level 1-2 to Level 5 after one scaffolding question — qualifying as Level 3 progression (scaffolded then independent). Subsequent Level 5 moments were unscaffolded. The independence criterion is partially satisfied: the student needed one scaffold to reach the programmability definition but then operated independently. The generative reasoning criterion is partially satisfied (EVM-covers-all-transactions inference was genuinely the student's own). The weak misconception about PoW-as-primary-distinction does not cap below 0.6. Trajectory is improving, which weights the late Level 5 evidence more heavily.

Misconceptions: None identified. The errors (wrong contract name, redundant line) were surface-level copy issues, not conceptual misunderstandings about what a sequence diagram represents.

Assessment rationale: First exposure procedural concept. Evidence was Level 1 (passive confirmation of arrow semantics) and Level 2 (scaffolded correction of two specific errors). Trajectory: Declining on the specific Sepolia-vs-Hardhat question. Strong start (correctly named testnet and local environment independently), then produced an incorrect answer, then admitted uncertainty. The Companion supplied the resolution.

Assessment rationale: First exposure procedural concept. The student showed Level 5 on the basic pipeline (testnet exists, local environment exists before testnet) but a weak persisting misconception about Sepolia's function and an explicit admission of non-understanding on the Hardhat-vs-Sepolia distinction. The misconception constraint (cap at 0.6) applies at the weak level, but the score would fall below this cap regardless given the flat-to-declining trajectory on the specific knowledge. Calibration anchors for first exposure with mixed evidence suggest 0.25–0.40; the early Level 5 moments push slightly upward, the declining trajectory and unresolved misconception push downward.

Layer-to-function assignments (reliable delivery → Transport, not Application; DHT → Internet, not Link) required scaffolding and were not self-corrected, representing Level 2 moments on specific knowledge.
computer_networks_fundamentals.source_notes: Articulated the modularity benefit of layered architecture independently and correctly ('changing one layer doesn't force changes in others'). Consistently misassigns specific functions to layers — placed reliable delivery at Application, DHT at Link — both requiring Companion correction. Next session should present a new scenario (e.g., video streaming) and ask the student to assign which layer handles which aspect before any scaffolding, to test whether the layer-function mapping was retained.

Self-correction was substantive, not just answer replacement, but was triggered by a single prompt rather than arising unprompted.
bitcoin_as_case_study.source_notes: Initially imported PoW-as-always-necessary framing before recognizing the identity/accountability distinction with one prompt. Once oriented, correctly articulated why PoW is unnecessary in permissioned contexts (known identities, auditability, alternative consensus mechanisms). Next session should present a new scenario — e.g., a consortium of banks vs. a public DeFi protocol — and ask the student to reason from scratch which design choices are appropriate and why, without any scaffolding.

consensus_mechanisms.evidence_summary: Student could not arrive at PoW or PoS independently (Level 2 early, required the Companion to introduce both). After introduction, demonstrated Level 5 reasoning: correctly connected fraud attempts to economic loss across both mechanisms, and independently mapped PoW vs. PoS trade-offs including the PoS centralization concern. Trajectory improved from scaffolded start to independent analysis after concept introduction.
consensus_mechanisms.source_notes: Could not generate the PoW/PoS mechanisms without the Companion introducing them, but once introduced, correctly identified the PoS centralization-of-power concern independently — a non-trivial insight. Summary of PoS as 'mais rede, mais poder' slightly imprecise (stake, not network size). Next session should test whether the student can name and explain both mechanisms from scratch, and ask them to construct a scenario where PoS centralization creates a specific problem.

Trajectory improved from one scaffolded moment to consistent independence. Weak misconception about hash invertibility was functionally resolved but not theoretically explained.

Constructed an original conceptual model of hash-like character dependency. Next session should open by asking the student to explain why hashes cannot be reversed.

mining.evidence_summary: Persistent misconception about the nonce's role (described as 'giving access' or 'granting rights' to block data) required two corrections and multiple scaffolding steps to resolve procedurally. The procedural mechanics (what validators calculate, what the hash target looks like) were reached only through scaffolding. Contrasting strength: the incentive and game-theory reasoning at the end was Level 5 independent, with the game-theory invocation being the student's own connection.
mining.source_notes: Has the incentive logic (economic cost, game-theoretic framing) firmly in hand — invoked game theory independently and correctly. Procedural mechanics are shaky: described the nonce as 'giving access to block data' twice before being corrected, and needed step-by-step scaffolding to correctly describe what validators do with the provided nonce. Next session should ask the student to describe what a miner does step by step — specifically what the nonce is, what it is combined with, and what condition the resulting hash must satisfy — before any new material.

blockchain_trilemma.evidence_summary: One strong Level 5 moment (independently traced scalability → synchronization risk → security risk), followed by a weak persisting misconception (decentralization reduces security via reversibility framing), followed by Level 2 scaffolded recovery. Trajectory mixed; the misconception was corrected by the Companion but not resolved by the student.
blockchain_trilemma.source_notes: Conflated decentralization with reduced security by importing the reversibility framing from prior sessions — stated 'descentralização = falta de segurança,' which inverts the correct relationship. Companion corrected this, but the student's recovery was a scaffolded echo. Can trace the scalability-security tension independently but does not yet have a stable model of where decentralization sits in the trilemma. Next session should ask the student to explain the trilemma in their own words before any scaffolding, specifically probing the decentralization-scalability (not security) link.

game_theory_and_trust.evidence_summary: Student produced Level 5 reasoning on detection probability independently, reached penalty logic after one scaffolded prompt, and generated a compressed but accurate synthesis ('o desonesto sai no prejuízo') that qualifies as Level 6 — their own formulation of the rational-choice incentive structure. Trajectory improved; the generative closing synthesis is the strongest signal.
game_theory_and_trust.source_notes: Student synthesized the game-theoretic incentive logic independently as 'o desonesto sai no prejuízo' — a correct compressed formulation that was their own, not the Companion's phrasing. Reasoning about penalty was only partially arrived at independently (required one scaffolding prompt to reach the economic cost angle). Next session should probe whether the student can articulate the distinction between 'hard to get away with' (detection) and 'not worth trying' (economic penalty) as separate mechanisms, since the session showed the student defaults to the detection framing first.

hash_tables.evidence_summary: First exposure concept with an improving trajectory: initial misconception (linked-list model) was replaced through scaffolded execution (Level 2-3 on linear probing), and the student independently articulated the O(1)-when-no-collisions condition (Level 5) without prompting. The core trade-off (speed vs. collision cost) was grasped independently; the deeper dependency on hash function quality was introduced but not tested.
hash_tables.source_notes: Can execute linear probing for insertion and search step-by-step, and independently stated that O(1) performance holds only when collisions are absent. Did not independently identify hash function quality as the fundamental design dependency — that was Companion-supplied. Next session should open by asking the student to describe the hash table mechanism and the conditions under which it is and isn't fast, without scaffolding, to verify retention of the collision-cost trade-off.

blockchain_types.evidence_summary: Level 5 on public/private distinctions and the absence of proof-of-work in permissioned chains; Level 6 on the hybrid model — student independently generated the central bank digital currency use case (Real Digital), which the Companion confirmed as accurate. One Level 2 moment (imprecise framing of permissioned blockchain as 'auditors validating before upload') but followed by continued independent performance.
blockchain_types.source_notes: Generated the CBDC use case for consortium blockchain independently and accurately. Correctly identified that permissioned chains gain computational efficiency by removing proof of work. Framing of permissioned blockchain as 'pre-upload auditor validation' rather than 'restricted write access' suggests the structural definition may be slightly imprecise — next session should ask the student to define the three types in their own words before any scaffolding to verify the distinctions held.

proof_of_work.evidence_summary: Consistent Level 5 independent responses throughout: correctly described brute-force nonce search, identified the combinatorial difficulty, characterized the attacker's required effort, and grounded security in economic cost — all without scaffolding. One minor inversion ('asymmetry makes validation hard') was corrected by the Companion and not self-corrected, but surrounding evidence suggests it was a phrasing error rather than a structural misconception.
proof_of_work.source_notes: Correctly and independently described PoW as brute-force nonce search, identified that finding is expensive while verifying is trivial, and grounded the security argument in economic cost without prompting. Did not demonstrate the difficulty-adjustment mechanism or the 51% attack framing in this session. Next session should ask the student to explain what happens to the leading-zeros target as the network's computational power grows, to test whether the difficulty-adjustment concept is accessible from their current model.

asymmetric_cryptography.evidence_summary: Multiple Level 5 moments across the second half of the session (symmetric vs. asymmetric distinction, key-distribution mechanism, paired-key generalization), with a partially-scaffolded arrival on the signature direction and one corrected surface error on the combined sigilo+autenticidade scenario. Trajectory was improving with strongest evidence in the latter half.
asymmetric_cryptography.source_notes: Can explain the symmetric/asymmetric distinction and the key-distribution solution independently and in own words. Formed a correct paired-key generalization ('ambas as chaves podem ser usadas para criptografar e descriptografar') without prompting. Still confused about when to use each key for authentication vs. confidentiality under novel combined scenarios — inverted remetente/destinatário in the final test and needed correction. Next session should present a fresh authentication-only scenario and ask the student to reason through which keys to use before any scaffolding, to test whether the paired-key model is stable.

blockchain_fundamentals.evidence_summary: Session opened with a direct test of the prior session's noted gap (PoW cost mechanism). Student produced Level 1 restatement of the chain-break effect, then a Level 2 response with a reactivated hash-invertibility misconception ('decifrar'), then a Level 2 response with an incorrect target description ('igual ao id do bloco'). All corrections were supplied by the Companion. Trajectory was flat-weak; the prior session score of 0.55 is not supported by this session's evidence.
blockchain_fundamentals.source_notes: The hash invertibility misconception (framing proof-of-work as 'deciphering' a hash) persisted from Session 1 and reappeared unprompted. Student described the brute-force iteration correctly but named the target incorrectly — 'matching the block ID' rather than 'satisfying a leading-zeros condition.' Next session should ask the student to explain step-by-step what a miner does when solving a block, without any scaffolding, before introducing new material.

distributed_systems_fundamentals.evidence_summary: Multiple Level 5 responses produced independently (consensus, redundancy, institutional trade-offs) and two Level 6 moments (unprompted theory-of-games reference as adversarial framing; original PIX fraud analogy illustrating irreversibility). Trajectory was strongly improving across the session with no evidence of scaffolding dependence.
distributed_systems_fundamentals.source_notes: Generated the irreversibility trade-off independently using a PIX fraud example, and connected adversarial incentives to game theory without prompting. Can articulate decentralization, redundancy, and irreversibility correctly. Final synthesis ('você ganha segurança e perde suporte') was under-specified relative to earlier reasoning — next session should probe whether the student can articulate the autonomy/reversibility trade-off without the vocabulary being provided.

digital_signatures.evidence_summary: Multiple Level 5 independent moments (efficiency rationale, confidentiality limitation) and one solid procedural reconstruction after minimal scaffolding, producing an improving trajectory across the session. A weak persisting misconception about the internal verification mechanics (what exactly is 'decrypted' in the comparison step) was partially addressed but not self-corrected by the student.
digital_signatures.source_notes: Arrived at the session with prior asymmetric-cryptography knowledge that let them produce a near-complete description unprompted. Correctly identified the efficiency rationale for signing the hash (not the message) and independently stated that signatures don't guarantee confidentiality. Verification mechanics are slightly muddled — described 'decrypting the hash of the transaction' rather than the cleaner model of decrypting the signature to recover the signer's hash, then independently hashing the received message and comparing. Next session should ask the student to walk through verification step by step without scaffolding, specifically probing what the two objects being compared are and where each comes from.

cryptography_introduction.evidence_summary: Student showed genuine Level 5 independence early (encryption definition, hash/encryption distinction), then regressed into a key-as-hash misconception requiring multiple scaffolding steps to resolve, closing with passive acknowledgment. Trajectory was declining after the early peak — the session probed deeper and the student's independent understanding did not hold.
cryptography_introduction.source_notes: Can articulate what encryption does (transforms text irreversibly-for-outsiders) and correctly distinguishes it from hashing, but formed a confused model of what a key *is* — twice described the key as a hash, and could only abandon this framing through scaffolding, not through independent reasoning. Next session should ask the student to define 'chave criptográfica' in their own words before any new material, specifically probing whether they can separate the key (parameter) from the transformation (algorithm) without prompting.

symmetric_cryptography.evidence_summary: Only one substantive engagement — a Level 1 echo of the Companion's definition of 'simétrica.' Student explicitly could not engage with the key-distribution problem. No independent reasoning demonstrated on this concept.
symmetric_cryptography.source_notes: Symmetric cryptography was introduced and immediately defined by the Companion; student echoed the definition without demonstrating understanding. Could not engage with the key-exchange problem at all. Next session should ask the student to explain what symmetric cryptography is and why it has a fundamental limitation, before any scaffolding — this will reveal whether the echo-level exposure produced any retention.

distributed_hash_tables.evidence_summary: First exposure concept with mixed evidence: student independently arrived at hash-based assignment (Level 5) and applied it to the BitTorrent piece-lookup scenario without scaffolding (Level 5), but the consistent hashing ring model was only passively acknowledged and a weak misconception about BitTorrent's purpose (memory distribution vs. bandwidth/availability) was corrected but not confirmed resolved.
distributed_hash_tables.source_notes: Independently connected hash identity to node responsibility ('the computer with the matching hash stores the book') and applied DHT logic to BitTorrent piece lookup without prompting. Does not have a clear model of the consistent hashing ring — understood it when explained visually but did not engage with it independently. Next session should ask the student to explain how DHT decides which node is responsible for a resource, and what happens when a node leaves the network, before any scaffolding.

ethereum_platform.evidence_summary: Opened with a weak misconception (PoW cost as the primary Bitcoin-SC barrier) and a wrong guess on programmability (open source), but after a single scaffolding question produced a correct independent definition of programmability, followed by multiple unscaffolded Level 5 responses on EVM scope, gas mechanism, and economic disincentive logic. Trajectory strongly improving; later evidence dominates.
ethereum_platform.source_notes: Can define programmability in own terms ('nodes execute functions created by users'), correctly inferred that the EVM handles all Ethereum transactions (not just contracts), and independently connected gas to economic disincentive for infinite loops. The Turing-incompleteness of Bitcoin's scripting language was not independently articulated — the student's model attributes the difference primarily to PoW cost rather than to architectural design. Next session should ask the student to explain what makes Ethereum 'programmable' and what specifically Bitcoin lacks, without scaffolding, to verify the programmability concept held.

byzantine_generals_problem.evidence_summary: Student produced two Level 5 responses without scaffolding: independently arrived at the majority-threshold intuition, and invoked the 2/3 threshold by name before the Companion stated it. Also independently identified financial incentive as the motive for Byzantine behavior. One passive acknowledgment moment mid-session was the weakest point. Trajectory improved across the session.
byzantine_generals_problem.source_notes: Independently invoked the 2/3 threshold and identified financial incentives as the driver of dishonest behavior — both unprompted. Did not demonstrate the procedural mechanics (how a BFT algorithm actually produces consensus across rounds), and the 1/3 fault tolerance limit was supplied by the Companion rather than derived by the student. Next session should ask the student to work through a four-node example with one traitor to test whether the threshold reasoning transfers to procedure.

sequence_diagrams.evidence_summary: Evidence was Level 1 (confirmed arrow semantics without elaboration) and Level 2 (produced diagram by following template, corrected two naming/redundancy errors when prompted). No independent reasoning beyond template application was demonstrated; the concept was covered briefly with a low-complexity task. Trajectory was flat.
sequence_diagrams.source_notes: Can replicate a sequence diagram template for a known contract after being shown the format — correctly maps actors to contract methods and internal state changes. Made two surface errors (wrong contract name, spurious line) that required Companion identification before correction. Has not demonstrated understanding of when to use sequence diagrams, what design decisions they reveal, or how to produce one for a novel scenario. Next session should present a new interaction (e.g., a two-contract system) and ask the student to draw the diagram without a template, to test whether the format was understood or only imitated.

p2p_networks.evidence_summary: First exposure with Level 5 on P2P structural definition (decentralized, no central server, nodes know neighbors) and a strong Level 5 closing synthesis connecting hash tables → DHT → P2P independently. The bootstrap discovery problem was not independently processed — defaulted to geographic proximity, required Companion correction, and the student confirmed the bootstrap node was their main difficulty for the session.
p2p_networks.source_notes: Can define P2P correctly and connect it to DHT in their own synthesis. The bootstrap node mechanism — a new node entering a decentralized network via a hardcoded entry point — was not independently understood; the student's geographic-proximity fallback suggests the underlying IP-layer resolution was not grasped. Next session should ask the student to explain how a new node joins a P2P network from scratch, specifically probing the bootstrap mechanism, before introducing any new material.

hash_functions.evidence_summary: One Level 5 moment early (independent correct statement that hashes cannot be reversed), with no further coverage of the concept in this session. No opportunity to assess depth, mechanism, or properties beyond directionality.
hash_functions.source_notes: Correctly and independently stated that hashes are irreversible, which is an improvement over the prior sessions where this was confused. However, prior profile notes the student cannot explain *why* hashes are irreversible — that gap was not addressed this session. Next session should open by asking the student to explain what a hash function does and why it cannot be reversed, before introducing any new cryptography material.

smart_contracts.evidence_summary: Student produced Level 6 generative reasoning in the opening response before any instruction — independently describing escrow mechanics, multiple vulnerability classes, the theDAO case, and the code-author responsibility problem. No scaffolding was required at any point; trajectory was flat at the top.
smart_contracts.source_notes: Arrived with substantial pre-existing knowledge: correctly described reentrancy, access-control vulnerabilities, and the theDAO incident without prompting, and independently derived the trusted-auditor paradox. Conceptual depth is strong. Procedural depth on vulnerability mechanics (e.g., exactly how reentrancy exploits state) was named but not demonstrated step-by-step. Next session should probe whether the student can describe a reentrancy attack mechanically — what sequence of calls occurs and why the state-update order matters — to test whether the vulnerability naming reflects procedural understanding or pattern recognition.

The Companion supplied the resolution. Trajectory declining after the initial strong moments.
blockchain_dev_tools.source_notes: Correctly and independently named testnets (Sepolia specifically) and the local testing step in the deployment pipeline. Has a weak misconception about Sepolia's function — believed it requires real gas payment, and after correction could not articulate why Sepolia adds value over a local simulator. Next session should ask the student to explain the full deployment pipeline and specifically why a developer would use Sepolia rather than stopping at Hardhat, before any scaffolding, to test whether the Companion's explanation of 'real conditions without real stakes' was retained.

solidity_basics.evidence_summary: Level 5 on contract comprehension (read the Cofre contract and identified its behavior independently) and access-control identification (immediately named require as the access-control mechanism). Contract writing required two scaffolded correction cycles — the structural pattern was grasped but variable-name consistency and syntax details (payable, semicolons) needed repeated prompting. Trajectory improving but not fully independent on production.
solidity_basics.source_notes: Can read and interpret a Solidity contract independently, correctly identifying what each function does and which line enforces access control. Writing a contract from scratch showed correct structural understanding (constructor sets owner, require enforces access) but implementation details needed correction across two rounds (wrong variable names, inappropriate payable flag, missing semicolon). Next session should ask the student to write a new contract from scratch — e.g., a simple voting contract where only the owner can add candidates but anyone can vote — without seeing any template, to test whether the write pattern was internalized or only pattern-matched.
